Original Text:
Cars. Cars have been around since they became famous in the 1900s, when Henry Ford created and built the first ModelT. Cars have played a major role in our every day lives since then. But now, people are starting to question if limiting car usage would be a good thing. To me, limiting the use of cars might be a good thing to do.

In like matter of this, article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," by Elizabeth Rosenthal states, how automobiles are the linchpin of suburbs, where middle class families from either Shanghai or Chicago tend to make their homes. Experts say how this is a huge impediment to current efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from tailpipe. Passenger cars are responsible for 12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe...and up to 50 percent in some carintensive areas in the United States. Cars are the main reason for the greenhouse gas emissions because of a lot of people driving them around all the time getting where they need to go. Article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," by Robert Duffer says, how Paris, after days of nearrecord pollution, enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city. It also says, how on Monday, motorist with evennumbered license plates were ordered to leave their cars at home or be fined a 22euro fine 31. The same order would be applied to oddnumbered plates the following day. Cars are the reason for polluting entire cities like Paris. This shows how bad cars can be because, of all the pollution that they can cause to an entire city.

Likewise, in the article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," by Andrew Selsky says, how programs that's set to spread to other countries, millions of Columbians hiked, biked, skated, or took the bus to work during a carfree day, leaving streets of this capital city eerily devoid of traffic jams. It was the third straight year cars have been banned with only buses and taxis permitted for the Day Without Cars in the capital city of 7 million. People like the idea of having carfree days because, it allows them to lesson the pollution that cars put out of their exhaust from people driving all the time. The article also tells how parks and sports centers have bustled throughout the city uneven, pitted sidewalks have been replaced by broad, smooth sidewalks rushhour restrictions have dramatically cut traffic and new restaurants and upscale shopping districts have cropped up. Having no cars has been good for the country of Columbia because, it has aloud them to repair things that have needed repairs for a long time, traffic jams have gone down, and restaurants and shopping districts have popped up, all due to the fact of having less cars around.

In conclusion, the use of less cars and having carfree days, have had a big impact on the environment of cities because, it is cutting down the air pollution that the cars have majorly polluted, it has aloud countries like Columbia to repair sidewalks, and cut down traffic jams. Limiting the use of cars would be a good thing for America. So we should limit the use of cars by maybe riding a bike, or maybe walking somewhere that isn't that far from you and doesn't need the use of a car to get you there. To me, limiting the use of cars might be a good thing to do.
Rewritten Text:
Since their rise to prominence with Henry Ford's Model T in the early 1900s, automobiles have become integral to our daily lives. However, there is a growing debate about the potential benefits of restricting car usage. I believe limiting car usage is a step in the right direction.

Elizabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," highlights how car dependency, particularly in suburbs, where many middle-class families in places like Shanghai and Chicago reside, significantly hinders efforts to curb greenhouse gas emissions from vehicles. In Europe, passenger cars account for 12 percent of these emissions, a figure that can reach 50 percent in car-intensive regions of the United States. The sheer volume of cars on the road is a primary contributor to greenhouse gas emissions. Similarly, Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," details how Paris implemented a partial driving ban in response to severe pollution. Motorists with even-numbered license plates were prohibited from driving on one day, with odd-numbered plates facing the same restriction the following day, enforced by a 22-euro fine. This illustrates the severity of car pollution's impact on major cities.

Andrew Selsky's piece, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," describes a successful program where millions of Columbians chose alternative transportation like walking, biking, skating, or buses during a car-free day. This initiative, now in its third year and potentially expanding to other countries, transformed the streets of the capital, typically plagued by traffic congestion. The reduced pollution is a major benefit, and the article further notes improvements like sidewalk renovations, eased traffic during peak hours, and the emergence of new restaurants and shopping areas. The reduction in car traffic has yielded positive results for Columbia, from infrastructure improvements to new business opportunities.

In conclusion, decreased car usage and car-free initiatives have demonstrable positive effects on urban environments, primarily through reduced air pollution. These actions have also enabled cities like Bogota to address infrastructure needs and alleviate traffic congestion. Limiting car use in America could be beneficial. Perhaps we should consider biking or walking for shorter trips, reducing our reliance on cars. To me, limiting the use of cars might be a good thing to do.

Original Text:
Transportation is a large necessity in most countries worldwide. With no doubt, cars, buses, and other means of transportation make going from place to place easier and faster. However there's always a negative pollution. Although mobile transportation are a huge part of daily lives, we are endangering the Earth with harmful greenhouse gases, which could be suppressed.

A small suburb community in Germany called Vauban, has started a "carfree" lifestyle. In this city, markets and stores are placed nearby homes, instead of being located by farend highways. Although Vauban is not completely carfree, 70% of Vauban families do not own cars Even a large 57% of families stated to have sold their cars to move to Vauban. Some families have even said to be less stressed depending on car transportation. Cars are responsible for about 12% of greenhouse gases, and can even be up to 50% in some carintensive areas in the United States.

Another insight to reduced car zones brings Paris' incident with smog. Paris' officials created a system that would in fact lower smog rates. On Monday, the motorists with evennumbered license plates numbers would be ordered to leave their cars at home, or they would suffer a fine. Same rule would occur on Tuesday, except motorists with oddnumbered license plates were targeted with fines. Congestion, or traffic, was reduced by 60% after five days of intense smog. Diesel fuel played a huge part in this pollution, having the fact that 67% of vehicles in France are of Diesel fuel. The impact of the clearing of smog, resided in banning the Tuesday rule of odd license plates.

Could you imagine a day without seeing a single car being used? This phenomenon occurs once a year in Bogota, Colombia. With the exception of buses and taxis being used, cars are to be left unattended for an entire day. Having a carfree day just once a year can even reduce the pollution slightly. The day without cars is part of a campaign that originated in Bogota in the mid 1990s. This campaign has renewed and constructed numerous bicycle paths and sidewalks all over the city. Parks and sports centers have also sprung from this campaign. Devoting your time to a carfree lifestyle has it's hassles, but in hindsight, it has it's benefits.

To conclude, living a carfree lifestyle does not seem like a possibility in this day and age, however managing the use of cars and pollution is something every country should take time investing in. Think about how much of an impact it would be if everywhere worldwide would take part in airpollution reduction. Mobile transportation is lifestyle in a sense, and being dependent on cars or other means of transportation can impact the health of the Earth and even ourselves.
Rewritten Text:
Globally, transportation is undeniably essential, with cars, buses, and other vehicles facilitating movement with unprecedented ease and speed. Yet, this convenience comes at a significant environmental cost: pollution. While mobile transportation systems are integral to our daily routines, they contribute substantially to harmful greenhouse gas emissions, a problem that demands attention and mitigation.

In Vauban, a small German suburb, an innovative "car-free" lifestyle has taken root. Prioritizing accessibility, the community locates markets and stores close to residential areas, diverging from the conventional model of highway-adjacent commercial zones. While not entirely devoid of vehicles, a remarkable 70% of Vauban families forgo car ownership. Notably, a significant 57% of families reported selling their cars specifically to relocate to Vauban, with some even citing reduced stress associated with decreased car dependence. Considering cars account for approximately 12% of global greenhouse gas emissions, and potentially up to 50% in car-centric regions of the United States, the impact of such initiatives becomes evident.

Paris's experience with smog provides further insight into the potential of reduced car zones. Faced with severe air pollution, Parisian authorities implemented a policy restricting vehicle use based on license plate numbers. On designated days, motorists with even-numbered plates were prohibited from driving, while odd-numbered plates faced similar restrictions on alternate days, with fines imposed for violations. This measure resulted in a remarkable 60% reduction in traffic congestion within five days. Given that diesel fuel powers 67% of vehicles in France, it was recognized as a major contributor to the pollution. However, smog reduction strategies saw a setback following the removal of the alternate day rule regarding odd number plates.

Imagine a world without cars, even for a single day. This reality materializes annually in Bogota, Colombia. Except for buses and taxis, personal vehicles are banned from the streets for an entire day. Even a single car-free day can measurably reduce pollution. This initiative, originating in Bogota in the mid-1990s, has spurred the creation and improvement of numerous bicycle paths and sidewalks throughout the city. Parks and recreational centers have also flourished as a result. While embracing a car-free lifestyle may present challenges, its benefits are undeniable.

In conclusion, a completely car-free existence may seem improbable in the modern world, but managing car usage and pollution levels is a pressing responsibility for every nation. The cumulative effect of global participation in air pollution reduction would be substantial. Mobile transportation has become deeply ingrained in our way of life, but our dependence on cars and other vehicles has repercussions for both the health of the planet and our own well-being.

Rewritten Text:
Elisabeth Rosenthal suggests that America's long-standing devotion to the automobile may be waning, a trend that, upon closer examination, reveals potential benefits. It's not necessarily a decline, but more so a shift towards reduced car dependency, offering certain advantages for individuals and society. These advantages include enhanced safety and health, alongside reduced pollution and a lower degree of dependence on personal vehicles.

First, limiting car usage can contribute to increased safety and well-being. Feeling secure is a universal human need. In Bogota, Colombia, during a car-free day, Andrew Selsky observed that the streets were "eerily devoid of traffic jams." Traffic congestion introduces complications that undermine a sense of control; a simple journey becomes a source of stress and doubt. Reduced car traffic, conversely, minimizes these stressors. As Heidrun Walter noted, "when I had a car, I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Furthermore, diminishing reliance on cars encourages physical activity. While vehicles serve the crucial purpose of transportation, an over-reliance on them can encroach on leisure and fitness. Reduced car use necessitates walking, cycling, or using public transit, which promotes a more active lifestyle. Andrew Selsky reports that "parks and sports centers also have bloomed throughout the city" due to the lower traffic, further helping the fitness issue. In general, reducing car dependency can lower stress levels and improve overall physical health.

Second, decreased car usage offers notable environmental benefits. Despite some public cynicism towards environmental concerns, the underlying facts remain undeniable. Pollution is detrimental to the environment, which, in turn, negatively impacts human health, and cars are a significant contributor to pollution. As Elisabeth Rosenthal observes, a decline in car use "will have beneficial implications for carbon emissions and the environment." By reducing car usage, we can lower pollution levels and create a cleaner, healthier world for future generations. The detrimental effects of environmental pollution are undeniable, and car users bear a significant responsibility in addressing this issue.

Finally, limiting car usage can reduce our dependence on automobiles. While cars offer convenience and mobility, many individuals have become overly reliant on them. This reliance represents a departure from more natural and sustainable ways of living. As David Goldberg states, "all our development since World War II has been centered on the car, and that will have to change." Some might question the need for change, particularly given the perceived benefits of modern development. However, if development equates to dependence on a harmful machine, its effectiveness is questionable. Elisabeth Rosenthal observes that "cash-strapped Americans could not afford new cars, and the unemployed weren't going to work anyway," highlighting the limited access to private transportation for some. Those who have become accustomed to cars may be oblivious to the significant negative consequences of their use. By reducing our reliance on vehicles, we can mitigate these consequences.

In conclusion, the advantages of reduced car usage – enhanced health, decreased pollution, and lessened dependence – present a positive vision for a more organized, cleaner, and healthier society. In this regard, less reliance on vehicles can ensure the security and wellness of the vast majority.

Rewritten Text:
How often do you find yourself behind the wheel? Whether it's the daily commute, a trip to the grocery store, or a visit to the mall, have you ever considered how many people worldwide are doing the exact same thing – relying on fuel-powered vehicles for every journey? Few truly grasp the profound impact of simply turning the ignition key. Imagine, just for a moment, not driving to work today. For many dedicated car users, the immediate thought might be, "Impossible! I can't function without my car!" However, living with reduced car dependency is indeed achievable. Cities like Havana, Paris, and Bogotá have, in various ways, curtailed car use, and their residents are often better for it. Reducing your reliance on cars can yield numerous personal and communal advantages.

One often-overlooked benefit is the positive impact on your well-being. Heidrun Walter notes, "When he had a car, he was always tense. He's much happier without it." Consider the potential for road rage while driving; that stress cannot possibly be good for your health. Driving requires constant focus, from navigating traffic to dealing with inconsiderate drivers, leading to exhaustion and increased stress levels. Heavily populated cities like New York and Paris experience significant traffic congestion and pollution. Robert Duffer, in "Paris bans driving due to smog," explains how layers of warm air trap harmful car emissions. The health implications are obvious. Paris was even forced to implement a temporary driving ban due to record-high pollution levels, which decreased considerably after just a few days of restricted driving. With fewer cars on the road, people seek alternative transportation, such as walking, biking, or skating. All are forms of physical exercise, refreshing your body and mind more than simply sitting in a car.

Reducing driving can also significantly improve the appearance of your city. Pollution hinders plant growth, creates unpleasant odors, and casts a "dirty" haze over the entire area. Less driving translates directly to less pollution, fostering a cleaner, more appealing environment. In her article, "In German Suburb, Life goes on Without Cars," Elisabeth Rosenthal highlights that restricting car use encourages pedestrian traffic. More people on foot necessitates more sidewalks and closer, walkable stores. Andrew Selsky observes that, "Parks and sports centers have bloomed throughout the city... uneven pitted sidewalks have been replaced by broad, smooth sidewalks... and new restaurants and upscale shopping districts have cropped up." Because pollution is detrimental to the environment, organizations like the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency are promoting "car-reduced" communities, which, according to Rosenthal, boast far less pollution and greater cleanliness. Cities are also embracing the idea, organizing "no-car driving" days. Bogotá, Colombia, hosts an annual "car-free" day, permitting only buses and taxis, with hefty fines for unauthorized drivers. Although fining people for using their vehicles may initially seem controversial, the event has proven successful in significantly reducing smog. In conclusion, although relinquishing our cars and choosing to walk may seem unthinkable, it's not impossible. Reducing our driving can bring numerous benefits to our daily lives, leading to better health, a more aesthetically pleasing city, and a healthier environment overall.

Cars, undeniably, represent a remarkable feat of engineering and technological advancement. They efficiently transport us from one location to another, fulfilling our desire for swift travel. Cars can indeed be ideal for navigating cities in a matter of minutes, often surpassing the speed of other options. However, while their convenience is clear, it's worth considering whether their use contributes to stress and environmental concerns that we might overlook. Alternative forms of transport like buses or trains may offer a solution. Cars, while undoubtedly beneficial, might generate excessive stress and negatively impact the environment in subtle yet significant ways. Opting for buses or trains eliminates the worries of car maintenance or frustrating traffic jams. These options also reduce air pollution. Reducing our reliance on cars holds numerous benefits.

One key advantage, benefiting both people and the natural world, is a substantial decrease in air pollution. Currently, vehicles release harmful greenhouse gases through their exhaust systems. While a single car might not emit a significant amount, the collective impact of all vehicles is considerable, particularly in densely populated cities. For example, Paris experienced record-breaking pollution levels, prompting the city to implement temporary driving restrictions. As Robert Duffer notes in the article "Paris bans driving due to smog," a system was enforced that banned odd and even number plates to drive on alternating days of the week, if caught the driver was subject to a fine. Following these restrictions, congestion decreased by 60 percent. Similarly, Bogota, Colombia, hosts an annual car-free day, detailed in Andrew Selsky's "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," with the express purpose of reducing smog and promoting alternative transportation. Globally, there is a growing awareness that reducing car dependence is crucial for safeguarding environmental well-being.

Another compelling reason to limit car use is to mitigate stress. The act of driving is well-known to be a source of considerable stress. Traffic congestion, the constant need for car maintenance, and disruptive passengers all contribute to heightened stress levels. Limiting our reliance on cars could alleviate these stressors. Without the burden of driving, we would eliminate traffic jams, car washes, and enclosed-space disruptions. As Elisabeth Rosenthal reports in "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," a resident named Hartmut Walter stated, "When I had a car I was always tense. I’m much happier this way." Walter's experience suggests that reduced car dependence leads to decreased stress and increased happiness. Andrew Selsky, in his article on Bogota's car-free day, emphasizes that, "It's a good opportunity to take away stress..." When a chance to decrease stress presents itself, we should embrace it. Reducing stress provides a substantial advantage in our lives.

In conclusion, limiting car use offers significant advantages, most notably the protection of our environment and the reduction of stress. With our environment increasingly compromised, embracing practices that promote its recovery is essential. Stress is detrimental to personal health and well-being. If we aim to improve environmental conditions and reduce personal stress, we must adopt and implement this approach. While some may question its benefits, the evidence clearly demonstrates that limiting car use is an effective strategy for both environmental improvement and stress reduction, with proven results in various locations.

The electoral college system presents a fundamental problem: it undermines the principle of direct democracy. Citizens technically don't directly elect the president. Instead, they vote for electors pledged to a particular candidate, be it Democrat or Republican. As detailed in source two, these electors possess the power to choose an opposing candidate, effectively negating the votes cast by the populace. What, then, is the purpose of voting if not to select a leader who will safeguard our nation and foster opportunity? A key flaw lies in the "winner-take-all" rule.

Currently, Americans lack the power to directly choose their government, as source two clarifies that votes are cast for electors, each state given one vote, who then make the final presidential selection. Source two also notes the electoral college comprises 538 electors, requiring 270 votes to secure the presidency, and our number of electors aligns with the number of members of congress. Instead, I propose a system where the American people directly elect the president via a national popular vote. Eliminating the electoral college would ensure fairness, as every individual vote would contribute to a candidate's total, avoiding the potential for a tie. With the electoral college, a state's majority vote for a Republican candidate can overshadow the votes cast for the Democratic candidate, essentially rendering them meaningless. A popular vote system, however, guarantees that every vote counts, giving each candidate support from individuals, not just electors.

Source two highlights the concerning possibility that state-selected electors may choose an opposing candidate, disadvantaging the American people and jeopardizing America's future. A "faithless" Republican elector choosing the Democratic party would invalidate the votes of Republican supporters. Given the significance of this once-every-four-year decision, the American people should have full control in choosing their leader.

Sources two and three underscore the "winner-take-all" rule's inequity, with the winning candidate appropriating the votes of the losing side, despite those voters supporting a different candidate. It's as if money fell on the floor and someone just took it knowing its yours.

The "winner-take-all" system, further exacerbates this injustice by essentially confiscating the electoral votes of the losing candidates.

Rewritten Text:
Dear State Senator,

With the utmost respect, I am writing to advocate for a significant change in how we elect the President of the United States. Specifically, I believe a move away from the Electoral College towards a system more directly reflective of the national popular vote is warranted. As it stands, the Electoral College system introduces inherent unfairness, effectively allowing individual electors to wield disproportionate power over the will of the American people.

While revisions to the Electoral College may seem problematic at first glance, the potential benefits outweigh the drawbacks. A direct popular vote system for presidential elections could potentially incentivize candidates to offer financial incentives to voters, therefore it would make it difficult to create a fair election. Furthermore, direct democracy introduces challenges, such as resolving a potential tie and determining a fair tie-breaking mechanism. However, it's not about removing the Electoral College completely but modifying the way the electoral college vote.

The current system allows electors to cast their votes based on their own discretion, regardless of the popular vote in their state. The winner-take-all approach distorts the campaign landscape, compelling candidates to concentrate their efforts on a limited number of "swing" states. As evidence of this imbalance, consider the 2000 election cycle, in which seventeen states were virtually ignored by the major presidential candidates. This demonstrates the Electoral College as an outmoded system in dire need of replacement or, at the very least, substantial reform.

Currently, presidential candidates assemble a slate of electors rather than engaging with each state individually to secure votes. The candidate’s primary obligation, in essence, is simply to await the electoral vote count. This detachment creates a vulnerability; a candidate could conceivably circumvent the need for a broad, national campaign by directly influencing electors in key states through bribery or other means, effectively securing that state's vote without genuine popular support.

Voters, therefore, do not directly choose the president but, instead, select a slate of electors. These electors are chosen in various ways at state conventions or by the state party's central committee, and sometimes by the presidential candidates themselves. The most alarming aspect of the Electoral College is its vulnerability to potential crises. The system is exposed to factors that may lead to the election of someone other than the people's choice for President.

In conclusion, the Electoral College represents an antiquated method of presidential selection. By vesting ultimate authority in the hands of individual electors rather than the electorate, it contradicts the principles of modern democracy. Given the vast advancements in education and communication since its inception, the original rationale for electing representatives who then choose the president is no longer valid. A shift towards a more direct and equitable system is essential to ensure that the voice of the American people is accurately reflected in the selection of their highest leader.

Rewritten Text:
My fellow citizens, automobiles are undeniably central to modern life. While offering numerous benefits, curtailing their use presents compelling advantages, primarily in environmental health and reduced personal expense. Let's examine why embracing limited car usage is a prudent choice.

Paris, as reported in Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," implemented a partial driving ban to combat severe air pollution. This innovative scheme restricted driving on alternate days to vehicles with even or odd license plates, with violations resulting in fines. Simultaneously, public transportation became free. This initiative successfully reduced both traffic congestion and smog, benefiting the environment by lessening pollution in the ozone layer, while also offering a more economical commute. "Congestion was down 60 percent in the capital of France, after fivedays of intensifying smog...rivaled Beijing, China, which is known as one of the most polluted cities in the world". This demonstrates the severity of the pollution problem and the effectiveness of car usage restrictions in significantly improving air quality.

In Vauban, Germany, some residents have taken even more drastic steps, forgoing car ownership entirely. According to Elisabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, life goes on without cars," approximately 70% of residents do not own cars, often due to cost and the general prohibition within this experimental district. Many find life better without a personal vehicle. As Heidrun Walter, a Vauban resident, stated, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Moreover, as highlighted in Elisabeth Rosenthal’s "The end of car culture," younger generations are less inclined to drive or even obtain licenses compared to their elders. The focus has shifted away from the status symbol of car ownership towards simply achieving efficient transportation between destinations.

Ultimately, while cars provide transportation, numerous alternative options exist that are either free or less expensive, such as buses, trains, bicycles, and carpooling. These alternatives simultaneously lessen pollution and traffic congestion, promoting a healthier environment for everyone.

Rewritten Text:
The electoral college, as Plumer (Source 2) bluntly states, is "unfair, outdated, and irrational." This sentiment is widely shared, with many finding the system flawed to the point of being disastrous or simply illogical. While arguments exist for its retention – such as preventing runoff elections or protecting smaller states – these are increasingly outweighed by the compelling case for its abolishment, with a popular vote system being seen as a superior alternative.

One major grievance lies in the electoral college's inherent unfairness. It effectively concentrates voting power in the hands of a select group, diminishing the impact of individual votes. In contrast, a popular vote model ensures that every citizen's voice is equally valued, rather than being subject to the potential manipulation or even bribery of a few electors. The idea that these unelected officials hold disproportionate sway is a significant point of contention.

Its outdated nature is another common criticism. As Plumer (Source 2) quotes, "It's hard to say this, but Bob Dole was right: Abolish the electoral college!" Support for this position is growing, driven by the belief that the system is anachronistic and unfit for modern democracy. Some even suggest that, at times, a less sophisticated approach might yield better outcomes than the current process. As Plumer (Source 2) noted in 2000, "over 60 percent of voters would prefer a direct election," a figure that likely continues to rise.

Finally, the electoral college is often deemed simply irrational. Its convoluted structure raises serious questions about its logic. As Plumer (Source 2) explains, voters don't directly elect the president; instead, they choose electors who then cast the actual votes. The process by which these electors are chosen, their lack of direct accountability to voters, and the potential for voter confusion all contribute to a system that seems unnecessarily complex and undemocratic.

While proponents argue the electoral college offers certain benefits, the overwhelming sentiment, echoing Plumer's (Source 2) assessment, is that its inherent flaws outweigh any perceived advantages, particularly when a simpler and more equitable alternative – the popular vote – exists.

Rewritten Text:
Established by the founding fathers and enshrined in the Constitution, the Electoral College has been a fixture of American elections for centuries. Its continued use is warranted; having functioned effectively thus far, there's little justification for its removal. The Electoral College serves several crucial purposes, offering at least five distinct advantages that make it a valuable component of the electoral process.

However, the Electoral College isn't without potential pitfalls. Its effectiveness hinges on the unwavering loyalty of electors to their party, preventing them from exploiting their position to cast votes independently. Citizens technically vote for electors, who then choose the president. These electors, in theory, could disregard the popular vote and vote according to their desires. There is also the chance of states sending multiple slates of electors. While a direct popular vote would nearly eliminate the possibility of a tie, the Electoral College always presents this risk. Furthermore, candidates sometimes neglect states dominated by a single party, as the winner-take-all system diminishes the value of individual votes in these areas. In a direct election, candidates might be more inclined to campaign in these states to garner more votes. Despite these arguments against the Electoral College, these drawbacks are relatively infrequent and need to be weighed against its merits.

Swing states often become the focal point of presidential elections, and their voters are arguably the most informed. Knowing their votes will likely determine the outcome, they pay close attention to the news and the candidates' platforms. Swing states are showered with attention and information, as candidates seek their support. Conversely, many states are predictably aligned with one party, even before the election begins. Because voters in swing states are thoughtful of who will best represent our country, they are granted the privilege of making an impact during elections.

Moreover, the Electoral College can help prevent runoff elections by alleviating pressure in closely contested races. Imagine a scenario where the nation uses the direct popular vote system, with the Electoral College merely as a point of comparison. Should a close popular vote result in complications when compared with the Electoral College results, using the latter resolves any ambiguity. Examples like Nixon in 1968 and Clinton in 1992, who both won the Electoral College with only a 43% plurality of the popular vote, illustrate this benefit.

It is true that the Electoral College might alienate minority voters in states with strong partisan leanings. These voters might feel their individual vote has little impact on the overall outcome. While it is impossible for a single vote to overturn an election, and voter turnout wasn't complete in the 2012 election, voters are expressing their opinion rather than aiming to decide an election. A vote is more likely to hold meaning if a voter is educated on government happenings.

Rewritten Text:
Dear Senator, I urge you to maintain the Electoral College system. This system comprises 538 electors, requiring a majority of 270 to secure the presidency. Each state designates its own electors, chosen by the respective political parties of the candidates. Retaining the Electoral College is vital, ensuring definitive election results and guaranteeing the President represents the entire nation, not just specific regions.

One compelling reason to preserve the Electoral College is the assurance of a clear outcome. While a tie in the national electoral vote is theoretically possible, it remains highly improbable, despite the 538 electors constituting an even number. The 2012 election exemplifies this: Obama secured 61.7% of the electoral votes with only 51.3% of the popular vote, because states award electoral votes on a winner-take-all basis. Consequently, even a small lead in a state translates to a significant electoral victory within that state. The winner-take-all system directs candidates' focus to competitive "swing" states, rather than those where the outcome is predetermined. Nevertheless, the winning candidate's share of the Electoral College consistently surpasses their share of the popular vote.

Furthermore, the Electoral College ensures the President represents every citizen. This system necessitates candidates to cultivate broad, nationwide appeal. No single region possesses sufficient electoral votes to independently elect a president. For example, a strong regional favorite like Romney in the South gains no additional electoral votes by concentrating efforts in states he is already certain to win, diminishing the incentive to campaign heavily there. A president relying solely on regional popularity is unlikely to succeed, potentially alienating residents of other regions who may feel disenfranchised or unrepresented.

In summary, your support for the Electoral College is crucial. It provides a reliable path to avoid contested elections and fosters a presidency that serves the interests of the entire nation.

Dear Senator,

I've been considering the optimal method for electing our president and vice president, specifically the long-standing debate between the Electoral College and a national popular vote. Both systems have been in place for centuries, and it's crucial to determine which best serves our nation. Therefore, I'm writing to share my perspective and inquire about your preference, along with the reasoning behind it.

The Electoral College, as you know, involves 538 electors casting votes for the president and vice president. However, I find this system somewhat problematic. While we believe we are directly voting for a presidential candidate, we are in fact choosing a slate of electors who ultimately make the decision. I question the fairness of a system where a select group's opinions carry significantly more weight than the individual votes of millions of Americans. Why should a small number of electors hold such disproportionate power in determining the future direction of our country? It feels like a winner-take-all system.

In contrast, a popular vote system allows each citizen to directly participate in the election of the president and their cabinet. This gives us a genuine voice in shaping the nation's future. Even if our chosen candidate is unsuccessful, we still have the satisfaction of knowing our preference was recorded and contributed to the overall outcome. While the Electoral College might provide a seemingly more decisive result, the knowledge that our individual vote truly matters could incentivize greater civic engagement and more informed decision-making at the polls. It's empowering to know our voice is directly heard, rather than potentially overshadowed by a single electoral vote.

Admittedly, the Electoral College offers certain advantages. One is the potential for a clearer, more definitive outcome. Another could be the argument that electing electors is fundamentally the same as direct voting. However, these perceived benefits pale in comparison to the more significant advantages of direct participation through popular vote.

Ultimately, as you consider your stance on the presidential election, I urge you to reflect on how you believe votes should be voiced. Do you prioritize indirect representation through electors, or direct participation through individual votes? I believe the choice is clear: a system that amplifies individual voices is the most just and effective path forward.

Dear State Senator,

I am writing to advocate for the abolishment of the Electoral College, replacing it with a system of direct popular vote. My position is that the Electoral College is inherently unfair and presents a tangible danger to the principles enshrined in the U.S. Constitution.

The Electoral College undermines the democratic foundations established by our nation's founders. In a true democracy, the will of the people should prevail. However, when the popular vote determines one candidate as the choice of the American people, yet the Electoral College dictates a different outcome, the legitimacy of our democratic system is called into question. A clear example of this occurred in the 2000 election, where Al Gore won the popular vote, but George W. Bush ultimately assumed the presidency due to the Electoral College. As the article "The Indefensible Electoral College" points out, "Over 60% of voters would prefer a direct election to the kind we have now," demonstrating a clear preference among a majority of Americans for a system where every vote truly counts.

Moreover, the Electoral College poses a significant threat to the integrity of the Constitution and the rights of American citizens. The fundamental understanding is that citizens vote to elect the President. If the person chosen by the majority of voters does not win the election, it begs the question of where the democracy is in that process. The article "The Indefensible Electoral College" also highlights a potential crisis scenario, asking, "Oh what if a state sends two slates of electors to congress? It happened in Hawaii in 1960." Such a situation, where conflicting slates of electors are submitted, could lead to chaos and uncertainty, potentially jeopardizing the outcome of a presidential election. Fortunately, in 1960, then-Vice President Richard Nixon ensured that the slate of electors supporting his opponent was the one validated, but if this should happen again and go unnoticed, this could cause problems. The Electoral College is fraught with potential complications.

In summary, the Electoral College should be abolished due to its inherent unfairness and the threat it poses to the Constitution. I urge you to consider these points as you form your own conclusion on this important issue.

While some might argue that the Electoral College ensures a stable presidential selection process, critics counter that it can lead to situations where the popular will is disregarded. The system effectively mutes the majority opinion, leaving citizens feeling like their voices are inconsequential to the government's decision-making. Instances, such as Al Gore's loss despite winning the popular vote in 2000, as highlighted in "The Indefensible Electoral College", illustrate this disconnect and highlight public sentiment. In a nation supposedly built on the principle of the people's voice, the Electoral College stands in contradiction. Its existence obstructs a direct democracy where individual votes truly matter.
It is unfair, unjust, and unamerican. Let our individual voices be heard loud and clear in a system truly governed by the people.

Rewritten Text:
Dear Senator,

Many question the necessity of the Electoral College, arguing that it undermines direct democracy by prioritizing electors over individual voter preferences. While this sentiment is understandable, I believe the Electoral College continues to serve a vital function. Its effectiveness can be demonstrated through two key arguments: its ability to ensure a decisive outcome and its promotion of a president who represents the interests of the entire nation.

Firstly, the Electoral College provides greater certainty in election results. A transition to a purely popular vote system would likely be fraught with increased disputes and contested outcomes. As Richard A. Posner argues in his defense of the Electoral College, a winning candidate's share of the Electoral College vote invariably surpasses their share of the popular vote. This disparity reduces the likelihood of close, contested elections that could undermine public confidence. The Electoral College simplifies the process by delivering a clearer mandate. Furthermore, even potential popular vote ties, such as the hypothetical scenario of a deadlock between Nixon and Clinton in 1992, highlight the Electoral College's role in preventing prolonged uncertainty. The sheer volume of electoral votes, therefore, bolsters the certainty of the outcome compared to a simple popular tally.

Secondly, the Electoral College fosters a sense of national unity by encouraging candidates to appeal to a broad range of regions, rather than focusing solely on areas of strong support. Instead of catering to the specific needs of a single geographic region, a president elected through the Electoral College is incentivized to address the concerns of the entire nation. Consider, for example, a hypothetical scenario where a candidate primarily focuses on the Southern region due to its perceived Republican leanings. This approach would neglect the interests of other regions, such as the Northeast, Midwest, and Pacific Coast, which may hold different political priorities. While some, like Bradford Plumer, have criticized the Electoral College for encouraging candidates to ignore states they deem unwinnable, this argument fails to account for the dynamic nature of political landscapes. Plumer's pre-2004 analysis, for example, lacks the benefit of hindsight from subsequent elections. Examining the 2004, 2008, and 2012 elections reveals how the Electoral College compels candidates to address national trends and evolving voter demographics, necessitating a broader appeal than regional interests alone. The Electoral College, therefore, promotes the election of a president who is accountable to the nation as a whole.

In conclusion, the Electoral College should be retained due to its capacity to ensure a clear election outcome and to elect a president who represents the diverse interests of the entire nation. Its strength lies in its ability to produce decisive results and encourage a national perspective in our elected leader. I urge you, Senator, to support the continuation of the Electoral College, given its demonstrated effectiveness in past elections and its potential to ensure stability and representation in future contests.

Rewritten Text:
Once upon a time, before automobiles dominated our lives, people relied entirely on their own two feet. Daily life involved walking for everything – obtaining food, completing errands, and simply enjoying family time outdoors. This begs the question: why have cars become such an indispensable part of modern existence? The overuse of automobiles has prompted cities worldwide to enact regulations aimed at curbing their excessive use.

Since its inception, the automobile's most persistent challenge has been its environmental impact. Awareness of this issue has led to the creation of laws and initiatives aimed at reducing or eliminating car usage. The German community of Vauban, for example, has embraced a car-free lifestyle, with residents reporting increased happiness. As one Vauban mother of two, Heidrun Walter, stated, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way."

Paris, the "City of Love," has taken a dramatic step by banning cars to combat its severe smog problem. Remarkably, just five days after the ban was implemented, traffic congestion decreased by 60 percent.

Faced with the highest smog levels among European capitals, Paris had to act decisively to prevent the situation from worsening. The car ban has resulted in noticeably clearer air and a significant reduction in smog.

Even in densely populated cities like New York, designed with narrow streets, the advantages of alternatives to driving are becoming increasingly apparent. The city's successful bikesharing program reflects this shift. Nationally, carpooling initiatives encourage multiple individuals to share rides to work or school, reducing the number of vehicles on the road. This approach yields numerous benefits, including lower carbon dioxide emissions, decreased traffic congestion, and reduced fuel costs.

Bill Ford, Executive Chairman of Ford Motor Company, has even envisioned a future where personal car ownership is impractical. His proposal involves collaborating with the telecommunications industry to create interconnected urban networks that seamlessly integrate pedestrian, bicycle, private car, commercial, and public transportation traffic. The goal is to conserve resources, save time, enhance safety, and lower emissions. Although completely eliminating cars may not be realistic due to their convenience, we must significantly decrease our reliance on them. Overuse threatens to contribute to the greenhouse gases that imperil the planet. Solutions exist, however. Implementing a car-free day, and embracing alternatives, could make a substantial difference. Biking, for instance, provides an efficient and healthy means of transportation that combines exercise with getting to your destination.

America is all about democracy, so why don't the citizens get complete control by being able to elect their president directly? It's about time that the government finds a system that works better and gets rid of the Electoral College once and for all.

The electoral College has many flaws, a major one being that voters don't actually vote for their president, they vote for electors, and the electors are the ones who elect the president. You may ask yourself, "What's wrong with this? One way or another we're still choosing the candidate we want." While that is somewhat true, the system has many kinks that need to be fixed causing errors in the process. One example of this is the "winner takes all" rule. An excerpt from "The Indefensible Electoral College" says "... the electoral college is unfair to voters. because of the winner take all system in each state, candidates don't spend time in states they know they have no chance of winning, focusing only on the tight races in the "swing" states". Another huge problem with the Electoral College is called "the disaster factor". According to Bradford Plumer, " The American poeple should consider themselves lucky that the 2000 fiasco was the biggest election crisis in a century the system allows for much worse." Rewritten Text:
The Electoral College remains a deeply divisive element of presidential elections. While some defend it as a functional system, others view it as fundamentally flawed. Historically, the Electoral College emerged as a compromise, a middle ground between congressional appointment and direct popular vote. However, past elections have demonstrated its capacity to create problems, and the potential for future, even more severe crises looms large. In a nation that prizes democracy, the question arises: why are citizens not granted the direct power to elect their president? It’s time for the government to seek a superior system, one that ultimately supplants the Electoral College.

A primary flaw of the Electoral College is that voters do not directly elect the president. Instead, they vote for electors, who then cast the decisive votes. While one might argue that the intended candidate is still ultimately chosen, the system contains inherent weaknesses leading to errors. The "winner-takes-all" rule exemplifies this. As highlighted in "The Indefensible Electoral College," this system is unfair to voters. Candidates disproportionately focus on swing states with close races, neglecting states deemed unwinnable. Essentially, candidates concentrate their efforts on select regions, effectively abandoning others. The same article notes that "During the 2000 campaign 17 states didn't see the candidates at all..." This evidence underscores that the Electoral College is a flawed, unjust system demanding reform.

The "disaster factor" presents another significant issue. According to Bradford Plumer, Americans were fortunate that the election crisis of 2000 was not worse, emphasizing that "the system allows for much worse." He recounts an instance in Louisiana where the legislature nearly replaced Democratic electors with those opposed to John F. Kennedy, potentially diverting the popular vote. This illustrates the potential for corruption within the system. Other problems, like states sending multiple slates of electors or electoral vote ties, are also possible. While solutions for these scenarios exist, why navigate these complications when a new, improved system could be devised?

Richard A. Posner defends the Electoral College, arguing that it balances the power between larger and smaller states by strategically allocating votes. However, this supposed balance seems to merely shift the advantage, now favoring smaller states. Posner also suggests that voters in swing states are more engaged in campaigns. Yet, Plumer refutes this, pointing out that "... seventeen states didn't see the campaign at all, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the largest media markets didn't see a single campaign ad." The evidence is compelling: the Electoral College has exploitable loopholes, making it an outdated system ripe for replacement.

I firmly believe that the Electoral College should be abolished. The electorate should have direct control over the election of the president. If direct election is not immediately feasible, the existing system, due to its inherent corruption, demands immediate attention. Despite claims that the Electoral College functions adequately, the evidence suggests otherwise, necessitating either fundamental reform or complete replacement.

If we fail to mend or replace the fabric of the Electoral College, we risk facing problems that may become too large to manage in the future. While electors may currently consider the public's desires, history shows cases where they have prioritized their own agendas.

The Electoral College inherently undermines the power of the people. Although citizens cast their votes for their preferred candidate, the ultimate decision rests with the electors. An elector, should they choose, possesses the power to disregard the popular vote and select whomever they please. Given the disproportionate ratio of electors to citizens in some states, it feels undemocratic that the votes of a few carry more weight than the collective will of the populace. This can lead to voter apathy, with individuals feeling their vote is inconsequential and unable to guarantee their chosen candidate's victory. This sentiment is further exacerbated by candidates neglecting states deemed unwinnable, prioritizing larger states with more electoral votes. All of these factors can lead to the feeling that elections are not in the hands of the people and if people feel as if their vote doesn't matter, they don't vote.

Beyond diminishing the influence of the average citizen, the Electoral College is arguably inefficient. Despite the low probability, the possibility of a tie remains due to the even number of electoral votes. In such a scenario, the decision would fall to the House of Representatives, an entity that may not necessarily reflect the will of the people. Given the partisan nature of Congress, allowing them to select the president could be seen as unjust. It is also possible for a candidate to win the popular vote yet lose the election due to the Electoral College, as seen in the 2000 election with Al Gore. Such a flawed system is ripe with vulnerabilities and inefficiencies. These weaknesses must be addressed before the Electoral College can be considered a viable method for electing a president, or we risk facing further challenges down the road.

While the Electoral College may function, it is largely ineffective and doesn't serve the interests of the public. The vote for a candidate isn't in the hands of the people, but instead a minority of chosen electors. In a nation that prides itself on the power of the people, the people have surprisingly less power than one would think. We need to speak out against the overrated system of the Electoral College and develop a system that fits the needs of a true democracy like America should be.

Rewritten Text:
Despite calls for its abolishment, the Electoral College possesses more merits than flaws. Its inclusion in the Constitution serves a crucial purpose: it represents a carefully crafted compromise between a presidential election decided solely by Congress and one based purely on the popular vote of citizens. This system empowers both the general populace to voice their preferences and the elected representatives of each state to advocate strongly for their respective interests. Though often criticized, the Electoral College remains the most effective method for electing the President.

The Electoral College necessitates that a presidential candidate cultivate widespread, national appeal. For instance, a candidate like Rodney might be incredibly popular in the South, but the South alone lacks the electoral votes needed to secure a presidential victory. Consequently, while Rodney might not need to campaign extensively in the South, he would need to dedicate significant resources to campaigning in the North to improve his chances of winning those states. This is a valuable outcome, as it encourages the election of a president who enjoys broad support across the nation, enabling them to govern more effectively. While candidates tend to concentrate on swing states, voters in these states are more likely to be engaged and informed, recognizing their pivotal role in determining the election's outcome. Ideally, the most thoughtful and engaged voters should have the most influence on the outcome of the election.

Moreover, the Electoral College should be maintained because its outcomes generally reflect the will of the people. Although Al Gore won the popular vote in the 2000 election, George W. Bush won the electoral vote, but such a discrepancy has been rare, not occurring since 1888. Each party selects electors who are trusted to support the party's nominee, and this trust is seldom violated. Proposals to replace the Electoral College with a popular vote system overlook the potential for disputes over the popular vote, which could be more challenging to resolve than those arising from the Electoral College. Furthermore, while a tie in the nationwide popular vote is theoretically possible given the even number of electoral votes, it remains exceedingly improbable, having never occurred. The Electoral College also prevents situations where no single candidate receives a majority of the votes cast.

While it can be argued that the Electoral College discourages voters in states where their preferred candidate has little chance of winning – Republicans in California, for example – every vote contributes to their state's overall electoral vote count. Presidential election voters are often motivated by a desire to express their political views, rather than the belief that a single vote will decisively alter the election's outcome. The Electoral College has functioned effectively for over a century, and it will continue to do so if the nation's citizens continue to support it.

Rewritten Text:
Dear Senator,

The Electoral College, a long-debated aspect of our election process, presents a straightforward choice: maintain the current system or transition to a national popular vote, where the presidency is directly decided by the total votes cast by the people. I urge you to seriously consider adopting the popular vote method for several compelling reasons. One major concern stems from voter turnout, with evidence suggesting that "only one half of Americans are voting" (In Defense of the Electoral College section 23) because many believe their individual vote lacks significance, particularly in reliably red or blue states like Texas and California.

Furthermore, the winner-take-all system inherent in the Electoral College encourages candidates to prioritize "swing states" and heavily populated areas while neglecting others. "Seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the largest media markets didn't see a single campaign ad" (What is wrong with the Electoral College Section 13) This strategic allocation of resources and attention leaves voters in smaller states feeling marginalized and disincentivized to participate. 

I am aware of the arguments in favor of preserving the Electoral College. The founding fathers established it as a compromise, and some view it as a mechanism to give "qualified citizens in congress" (What is the Electoral College Section 1) a voice in the final outcome, thereby ensuring a degree of balance. Additionally, it could be argued that the Electoral College, though somewhat outdated, functions adequately because voters still influence the electors' choices, and these electors are expected to vote with honesty as to which candidate received the most electoral votes (In Defense of the Electoral College Section 15 & 16). However, even when electoral votes are decisive, the popular vote can still be higher than the electoral vote despite it being a rare occurance (In Defense of the Electoral College Section 16).

Despite the logic behind the Electoral College, I believe there are flaws to the system that are easily exploitable. As a reminder, the electoral process can be corrupted by those with harmful intent as seen when "segreagationists tried to replace Democratic Electors with other electors to try and go against Kennedy" (The Indefensible Electoral College Section 11) when John F. Kennedy ran for President. 

Ultimately, the winner-take-all dynamic creates an imbalance, leading candidates to concentrate their efforts and resources on larger states and battleground areas while overlooking others. Therefore, I firmly advocate for a shift to a popular vote system. It would empower voters across the nation, encourage greater participation, and reinforce the principle of "Power to the People," ensuring that the individual will have a more direct impact on who leads our states and the nation.

Furthermore, the Electoral College prevents the need for runoff elections. Runoff elections occur when no candidate secures a majority of the vote. As historical examples show, figures like Nixon in 1968 and Clinton in 1992 won Electoral College majorities (301 and 370 votes respectively) with only a 43% plurality of the popular vote. The Electoral College effectively establishes a clear winner, eliminating the pressures and potential complications that could arise from a runoff scenario.

Finally, the Electoral College mitigates the potential loss of political influence for larger states. Therefore, I strongly advocate for the continued use of the Electoral College. While potentially superior methods might exist in theory, the Electoral College remains the most functional and effective system currently available.

The act of voting is a significant responsibility, one that grants U.S. citizens a powerful voice in shaping their nation's future. This voice, particularly when electing the president, should be expressed solely through the popular vote. The people should have the exclusive right to choose their leader; no other method is as appropriate. The popular vote method stands as the superior option for presidential elections due to its inherent fairness and straightforwardness, particularly when contrasted with the complexities of the Electoral College.

Firstly, the popular vote is inherently more equitable than the Electoral College system. The process is simple: each citizen casts a vote for their preferred candidate, and the candidate with the most votes wins. There are really no ways to cheat this process. In this system, candidates ideally dedicate time to engaging with voters across a wider range of states. However, according to Bradford Plumer's analysis of the Electoral College, candidates often concentrate their efforts on "swing" states, neglecting those deemed unwinnable. This disparity is unacceptable; every state deserves the opportunity to directly engage with presidential hopefuls and understand their platforms. Plumer highlights that during the 2000 campaign, seventeen states saw no candidate visits, while voters in 25 major media markets were deprived of campaign advertising. How can voters make informed decisions about who should lead the country when they are effectively denied the chance to assess the candidates themselves?

Secondly, the popular vote possesses a clarity and simplicity absent in the Electoral College, a system prone to complications and potential crises. The popular vote method is direct and easy to understand. Citizens vote, the votes are tallied, and the candidate with the majority wins. Conversely, the Electoral College introduces numerous potential pitfalls. Plumer notes that in the event of an electoral tie, the election would be decided by the House of Representatives, with each state delegation casting a single vote. This means that Wyoming, representing 500,000 voters, would wield the same influence as California, representing 35 million. Such a scenario introduces significant complications, inequalities, and raises questions about representation. The popular vote bypasses these complex procedures, ensuring a more straightforward and transparent election process.

In conclusion, the popular vote is the better method for electing presidents, as it offers both enhanced fairness and reduced complexity. It empowers individual citizens to choose their leader without intermediaries, and it avoids the potential pitfalls and inequalities inherent in the Electoral College system. Voting is a serious civic duty that deserves respect and consideration. Every citizen's voice should be heard directly, without the filtering influence of representatives or the disproportionate power of certain states. It's the individual votes that truly matter.

The Electoral College offers a straightforward and effective voting method. As outlined by the Office of the Federal Register in "What Is the Electoral College," the system involves selecting electors, their subsequent vote for President and Vice President, and finally, the Congressional count of electoral votes. Posner, in "Five reasons to keep our despised method of choosing the President," argues that "The Electoral College restores some of the weight in the political balance that large states by population lose by virtue of the malapportionment of the Senate decreed in the Constitution." This is a valid point, as larger states with bigger populations often dominate presidential elections under a popular vote system, potentially overshadowing the concerns of smaller states.

Furthermore, the Electoral College can prevent the need for runoff elections, a situation that might arise under a popular vote system if no candidate secures a majority. For example, Nixon in 1968 and Clinton in 1992 received only a plurality of the popular vote, yet both achieved an Electoral College majority, precluding the need for a runoff.

In conclusion, I believe the Electoral College should be retained. Our current voting process works well, ensuring that populous states like Texas don't disproportionately influence elections compared to smaller states like Rhode Island. The Electoral College also avoids the possibility of runoff elections. Despite some perceiving it as outdated, the Electoral College remains a sound method of electing our president.

It's time to admit the truth: the electoral college is a fundamentally flawed system – unfair, archaic, and illogical. Most arguments defending it crumble under scrutiny, amounting to little more than unsubstantiated claims. Conversely, the objections raised against a direct popular vote are flimsy at best. It may be difficult to acknowledge, but Bob Dole was correct: The electoral college should be abolished. Indeed, Nixon, Carter, Dole, and even groups as diverse as the US Chamber of Commerce and the AFL-CIO have all, at one time or another, advocated for its elimination.

The core problem lies in the fact that citizens don't directly elect the president; they vote for electors. Even then, electors are not always guaranteed to vote in line with who the people favor. In the event of an electoral tie, the election is thrown to the House of Representatives, a body that doesn't proportionally represent the population. This means a voter's choice for electors doesn't guarantee a vote for their preferred presidential candidate, electors are not always honest, and this system is faulty and not efficacious. Furthermore, voters don't even get to choose these electors, raising serious questions about their loyalty, honesty, and commitment to upholding the will of the people. As seen in 1960, where segregationists in Louisiana nearly succeeded in replacing the Democratic electors to oppose John F. Kennedy, the system is vulnerable to manipulation. Such deception is undeniable; the electoral college is simply not a reliable foundation for our nation. We cannot rely on a system that could be swayed at any given moment.

Moreover, the prospect of the House of Representatives deciding a tied election only compounds the absurdity. Because each state gets a single vote, the representative from Wyoming, speaking for half a million voters, would wield the same power as California's 55 representatives, who represent 35 million. Is this supposed to be sensible? It's absurd. Given the increasing trend of voters splitting their tickets – voting for one party for president and another for Congress – a House-decided election is unlikely to accurately reflect the people's will. And while an electoral tie might seem improbable, history shows how close we've come. A small shift of voters could result in a tie. How can people still trust in this institution?

In conclusion, transitioning to a popular vote system for presidential elections would be a positive step for the country. The current system, where a vote for electors is not necessarily a vote for the desired candidate, and where the House of Representatives can disproportionately decide the outcome, is fundamentally unjust. Voters deserve to have their voices heard and their votes accurately reflected, free from deception and indirect representation.

Last summer's trip to the West Coast, specifically California, was something I'd anticipated greatly. I had always idealized the state, envisioning its diverse landscape from surfing beaches to snowy northern mountains. Upon arriving in Los Angeles, I expected the typical urban energy, akin to Miami perhaps. However, the pervasive smog and relentless traffic were shocking, unfortunately obscuring the beauty of California for many and heavily polluting the air. This is a common issue in many cities worldwide heavily reliant on cars, where reducing car usage could make a far greater difference than commonly believed. Less air pollution and a safer environment for pedestrians and even drivers are clear advantages of limiting car dependency.

Elizabeth Rosenthal points out in a car-free cities analysis that "Passenger cars are responsible for 12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe... and up to 50 percent in carintensive areas of the United States" (Rosenthal, paragraph 5). This figure only partially illustrates the problem, as commercial vehicles and deliveries also significantly contribute. The concept of limiting car usage worldwide is gaining traction as awareness grows regarding the detrimental effects of greenhouse gas emissions on the Earth's atmosphere. Projects in nations such as France, Colombia, and the United States are making substantial efforts to reduce car use, and, according to another article by Rosenthal, these efforts are showing promise: "Recent studies suggest that Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses as each year goes by" (paragraph 29). While eliminating car usage entirely isn't realistic, reducing it can significantly contribute to safeguarding our planet's habitability.

Furthermore, decreased car use in areas densely populated by pedestrians, like New York City, could drastically reduce tragic car accidents. Approximately 500 traffic incidents occur daily in the U.S., and likely over 5,000 worldwide. Sadly, car accidents are a leading cause of death in many countries, often preventable. Complacency can arise when drivers possess excessive control behind the wheel, and the proliferation of cell phones and texting has exacerbated the problem. Encouragingly, programs implemented globally to reduce greenhouse gas emissions are also contributing to a reduction in car accidents. "Congestion was down 60 percent in the capital of France" (Duffer, paragraph 14). Source 3 also mentions that Bogota's Car-Free Day has inspired similar initiatives in other Latin American countries, aiming to alleviate traffic and improve pedestrian safety.

In conclusion, the importance of limiting car use globally is increasingly recognized and gaining momentum. Reducing car dependence mitigates air pollution, which poses a significant threat to human health, and diminishes the incidence of car-related accidents. On my California trip, I finally saw the state's beauty when I left the city and drove away from the crowded highways.

Rewritten Text:
Have you ever wondered about the causes of air pollution on our planet? Cars contribute significantly to the problem, but thankfully, reducing our reliance on them offers multiple benefits, including cleaner air, cost savings, and improved safety. Allow me to elaborate.

Firstly, limiting car usage can effectively combat air pollution. As evidenced in a recent event where "after days of near-record pollution, Paris enforced a partial driving ban," curtailing vehicle traffic can dramatically improve air quality in urban centers. The city's drastic measure underscores the severity of the issue and the potential for positive change. Furthermore, by reducing car dependency, we lessen the demand that fuels major energy companies' reliance on burning fossil fuels. Finally, diverse alternative transportation methods such as cycling, walking, public transportation, skateboarding, and subway systems directly contribute to cleaner air.

Another significant advantage is the potential for substantial cost savings. As a passage says "The goal is to promote alternative transportation and reduce smog. Violators are to face 25 fines". The expense of car ownership extends beyond the initial purchase price. Gasoline costs, which are constantly on the rise, represent a significant ongoing expense. Moreover, car owners must also budget for potential traffic violations and costly maintenance such as tire replacements, light repairs, and oil changes. By limiting car usage, individuals can experience notable financial relief over time.

Finally, reducing our dependence on cars enhances overall safety. As mentioned in a passage "pedestrian,bicycle,private cars,commercial and public transportation traffic are woven into a connected network to save time,conserve resouses,lower emissions and improve safety". Decreasing car traffic can mitigate congestion and, consequently, lower the incidence of fatal car accidents. Furthermore, limiting car usage safeguards vulnerable road users, such as motorcyclists, cyclists, and pedestrians, by reducing their exposure to vehicular traffic. It also helps minimize the risk posed by impaired drivers.

In conclusion, the advantages of reducing car usage are compelling. From slowing down air pollution and saving money to promoting safety, the benefits are undeniable. Having shared my perspective, I'm curious to hear your thoughts on the matter.

Now, the words of a fourteen year old might not mean much to a state senator, especially since it will be four years until I am legally allowed to vote, but please sir, hear me out. I believe that we should keep the Electoral College for many reasons. One, if the president was decided by popular vote, our president might not be the best one for the country. Secondly, if it were up to a popular vote, the president might win, or not win, simply for being a regional favorite.

To start this off, if our president was decided by a popular vote, our president might not be the best thing for our country. As stated in the third article, paragraph 20, "The winnertakeall method of awarding electoral votes induces the candidates... to focus their campaign efforts on the tossup states." As you know, tossup state's make the final vote. Therefore, people in these states are more likely to pay close attention to the campaign, and they will be the ones with the most information. If it were up to popular vote, then anyone over the age of 18 could randomly pick a winner. Let's be honest, the majority of eighteen year old's will vote on whoever their friends or family vote for, or even the candidate representing their group Democratic or Republic. Many people, if the president was decided by popular vote, would make an uneducated decision on one of the candidates, not studying their choices as much as one would with the Electoral College system in place.

Secondly, if it were up to a popular vote, the president might simply win because he or she is a regional favorite. As stated in the third article, paragraph 19, "...because a candidate with only regional appeal is unlikely to be a successful president." Also in the paragraph, it states that no region has enough electoral votes to elect a president. If the president was decided by popular vote, however, can you imagine what it would be like? Take rodney, for example. He was a solid regional favorite in the South. rodney knew that there was no incentive to campaign heavily in the South, because he would not gain any electoral votes by increasing his plurilaty in states he knew he would win. If popularity decided the president, residents of the other regions might feel like their vote would not count, and that the president would have no regard for their interests. Say that popularity did actually determine a winner. While most regions might vote for a certain presidents, the ones that did not would feel like that the man running the country wasnt really "their president". However, Mr. Senator, if the Electoral College was to stay, this problem would never exist. With the system we have now, the people vote for your state's electors when they vote for the president. Most states also have a "winnertakeall" system. Therefore, the canidate with the most votes in a state win's that state's electors. This lets the voting be even, fair.
