Original Text:
The electoral college is an unfair and outdated system. It is unfair to voters. A states electoral votes often do not tell what that state truly wants. And no candidate should face what Gore did in 2000, with a winning popular vote, but less electoral votes losing him the election. It is an outdated system that should not be used in today's modern society.

When we vote for a specific president, we are actually voting for a slate of electors. The chosen electors are supposed to support the winning candidate, but they can easily decide to ignore that and cast their vote toward whomever the please. This can cause a president to get the greatest popular vote, and still lose because they did not get as many electoral college votes once all states' votes have been combined. This should certainly not happen. Whatever candidate is preferred by the most people to take office should win the election, period. In the past, maybe it would have been good to let the more educated electors choose the president rather than any citizen, but in this modern age of knowledge, people can be trusted to choose a leader that shares in the best interests of our country. Al Gore in 2000 lost the election after winning the most popular votes. That shows us that this electoral system does not work effectively, and should not be used today.

Perhaps more worrying is the electoral college's winnertakeall system. If a candidate wins a state's election by a tiny amount, they get every electoral vote for that state. This allows candidates to ignore smaller states, or states they know they will win, and focus on larger states and ones that have a very tight election could be easily persuaded for advertisements and campaigns. Ohio is known as a state that looks at their candidates closely, and can be persuaded to choose one based on what they know about them. Candidates tend to focus more resources here or in other similar states. It makes some sense to focus more on larger populations, and states like Ohio that are more interested in the election, but during the 2000 campaign, seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all, and could not make educated decisions during the election. In a close election, half the people in the state's views will be ignored, and will not matter to the election. That means half of California's 35 million voters would not matter at all in the election. The fate of the election should not be put in the hands of Ohio or other "swing" states, just ignoring less important ones.

Today's society needs a more fair system of electing a president. A popular vote from everyone is the most fair way to make sure the most popular candidate wins the election, and will cause candidates to focus on all citizens, focusing on large populations, but still trying to gain popularity with rural areas as well. America's citizens deserve a fair, proper election, that satisfies the most people possible.
Rewritten Text:
The Electoral College is a system ripe for reform, a relic that no longer serves the interests of a modern democracy. It undermines the fundamental principle of one person, one vote, by distorting the will of the electorate. The outcome of the 2000 election, where Al Gore won the popular vote yet lost the presidency, stands as a stark reminder of this system's inherent flaws. No candidate should be denied the highest office despite securing the support of a majority of voters.

The process itself is convoluted. When citizens cast their ballots for a presidential candidate, they are technically voting for a slate of electors. While these electors are typically pledged to support the winning candidate, they possess the theoretical power to disregard the popular vote and cast their ballots as they see fit. A situation where the popular vote winner is denied the presidency due to the Electoral College is simply unacceptable; the candidate with the most votes should rightfully win the election. Perhaps in the past, it would have been beneficial to have a more educated elector pick the president, but nowadays people can be trusted to pick a leader that will support the best interests of the country.

The winner-take-all system, employed by most states, further exacerbates the problem. A candidate who wins a state by even the narrowest of margins receives all of that state's electoral votes. This encourages candidates to concentrate their resources on a handful of "swing" states, like Ohio, that are perceived as being up for grabs, while neglecting other states entirely. While it's understandable to prioritize states with large populations or a demonstrated interest in the election, the reality is that many states are effectively ignored during the campaign. During the 2000 campaign, 17 states were completely ignored by candidates, and that is unacceptable. This means that in the end, half the people in the state's views will be ignored, and will not matter to the election. The fate of the election should not be put in the hands of Ohio or other "swing" states, just ignoring less important ones.

A truly fair and representative system would be based on the national popular vote. Such a system would ensure that every vote counts equally and that the candidate with the broadest support wins the election. Candidates would be compelled to campaign in all regions of the country, addressing the concerns of both urban and rural communities. The American people deserve an electoral process that is equitable, transparent, and reflects the will of the majority.

Rewritten Text:
America's present-day electoral process relies on the Electoral College, a structure established by the nation's founders. This system involves the selection of electors, their assembly to cast votes for president and vice president, and the subsequent tallying of these votes by Congress. While some Americans defend it for providing definitive outcomes and preventing runoff elections, many others argue that it generates more problems than it solves. Among the challenges attributed to the Electoral College are the disproportionate focus on larger states, the potential for disruptive scenarios, and the "winner-take-all" approach, which can lead to perceived unfairness among voters.

The number of electors allocated to each state is determined by its congressional delegation size. Larger states generally possess a greater number of electors; for instance, Hawaii has four, while Texas boasts thirty-eight. This disparity often results in increased attention being given to populous states. Bradford Plumer highlighted this issue, noting that "each state casts only one vote, the single representative from Wyoming, representing 500,000 voters, would have as much say as the 55 representatives from California, who represent 35 million voters. Given that many voters vote one party for president and another for congress, the House's selection can hardly be expected to represent the will of the people".

The current electoral framework is susceptible to various disruptions, such as the 2000 election debacle where Al Gore won the popular vote but ultimately lost the presidency. Another concern revolves around the loyalty of electors to their pledged candidate. The possibility exists for an elector to defy their party's choice and cast a decisive vote for an opposing candidate, introducing an element of unreliability. Even more serious situations could occur, such as a state submitting two conflicting slates of electors, as happened in Hawaii in 1960.

Finally, the Electoral College's "winner-take-all" methodology contributes to its flawed nature. In most states, the presidential candidate who wins the popular vote receives all of that state's electoral votes. This practice incentivizes candidates to concentrate their efforts on closely contested "swing states," neglecting states where their chances of winning are perceived as slim. As Richard A. Posner pointed out, "During the 2000 campaign, seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the largest media markets didn't get to see a single ad."

In conclusion, the Electoral College is a questionable electoral system. It introduces numerous complications and, overall, treats voters unfairly. Prominent figures like Richard Nixon and Jimmy Carter have advocated for its complete abolition. The issues it creates, including unequal focus on states based on size, the potential for elector disloyalty, and the inherently unjust "winner-take-all" system, undeniably indicate that the Electoral College generates more problems than solutions.

"I couldn't be happier," someone exclaimed. What prompted such a declaration? Simply, giving up car ownership. Elisabeth Rosenthal highlights in her article that passenger cars contribute to 12% of Europe's greenhouse gas emissions, a figure that can reach 50% in more car-centric regions like the United States. Robert Duffer reported on Paris banning driving due to smog, noting that after a short period, pollution levels decreased and the ban was lifted. Reducing car usage offers the significant advantage of curbing these emissions, an incentive many would find compelling enough to consider the idea. Imagine the positive global impact if more communities emulated Vauban's approach!

For a holiday promoting car limitations, look no further than Bogota, Colombia. On this annual day, millions of Colombians walk, bike, skate, or use public transport to commute and conduct their day. The event is in its third year, and despite occasional rain, the mayor attests that participation remains high. Originating in Bogota, the concept is now spreading to other Colombian cities like Cali and Valledupar, and if it evolved into an international holiday, the benefits could be widespread. The reduced car traffic results in more pedestrians on sidewalks, prompting government investment in smoother, improved walkways. New restaurants, parks, sports centers, and even shopping complexes are emerging to cater to pedestrian traffic, enhancing quality of life. Expanding car-free initiatives to other areas could lead to a similar proliferation of new amenities and experiences, a significant advantage of reduced car dependence.

Elisabeth Rosenthal has also explored the idea that the United States may have reached peak driving. Data reveals that Americans are purchasing fewer vehicles, driving fewer miles on average, and obtaining fewer driver's licenses each year. Given the declining car usage in such a large population as the United States, shouldn't governments worldwide accelerate this trend through gradual, efficient car reduction policies? This could lead to increased socialization on public transport, as daily commuters become friends or acquaintances. In essence, limiting car usage might even foster new friendships!

From mitigating pollution to enhancing everyday life and even fostering social connections, the potential benefits of reducing car dependence are extensive.

The Electoral College, a system involving electors who cast votes for President and Vice President, with Congress ultimately counting these electoral votes, is a subject of ongoing debate. A central question is whether to maintain this long-standing process or transition to a popular vote system for presidential elections. In my view, the Electoral College should be abolished in favor of a national popular vote, as this would enhance fairness, diminish the likelihood of a tied election, and promote a more rational electoral process.

Currently, the Electoral College is neither equitable nor intuitive. Voters are not directly choosing their preferred candidate; instead, as indicated by Source 1 ("What is the Electoral College?"), they are selecting electors pledged to a specific candidate. Furthermore, the "winner-take-all" system in most states awards all of a state's electors to the candidate who wins the popular vote there. This indirect process introduces the possibility of voter confusion and unintended consequences, where a voter might inadvertently choose an elector who does not ultimately support their preferred presidential candidate. While some argue that parties select reliable electors who will vote according to the party's nominees, this does not eliminate the potential for confusion and mistaken choices that can undermine the intended outcome of the election.

Moreover, the Electoral College does not eliminate the potential for a tie. Source 2 ("The Indefensible Electoral College: Why even the best laid defenses of the system are wrong") reminds us that historical precedents exist, including instances where states have sent multiple slates of electors to Congress. Though infrequent, the possibility of a tie persists. In contrast, a national popular vote would mitigate this risk. Furthermore, the "winner-take-all" system leads candidates to concentrate their resources on a limited number of swing states. Source 2 highlights that a significant number of states have been effectively ignored by presidential campaigns, with some voters never even seeing campaign advertisements.

While there may be concerns about abolishing the Electoral College, the advantages of doing so outweigh the drawbacks. A popular vote system would simplify the process, making it more accessible and fair for voters. Concerns that smaller states might be overlooked are outweighed by the fact that voters can directly vote for who they want, not an elector. Ultimately, transitioning to a national popular vote would ensure that every vote counts equally and that the outcome of the election reflects the will of the majority of American voters. Therefor, the abolition of the Electoral College will provide the public with a more straight-forward selection.

Rewritten Text:
Cars are ubiquitous these days, a common fixture in our lives. Navigating from one place to another almost invariably involves using a car. However, an alternative transportation method is gaining traction: public transportation. Shifting towards public transportation offers numerous benefits, including reduced emissions, fewer vehicles clogging our roads, and overall improvements to city environments. This once-unlikely idea is increasingly coming to the forefront.

In densely populated cities like Chicago and New York, polluted air often casts a gloomy pall. A major contributor to this pollution is vehicle emissions. Curbing car usage would significantly help to clear the skies and mitigate air pollution, leading to a better quality of life for city residents. As an example, "after days of near-record pollution, Paris enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city." Paris implemented this ban because smog and pollution levels were becoming unmanageable. With fewer cars on the road, such problems would be far less prevalent in Paris and other cities worldwide.

Heavy traffic is a persistent problem on roads across the United States and globally. Reduced car usage could alleviate traffic congestion and enhance road safety. During the car ban in Paris, "congestion was down 60 percent in the capital of France, after five days of intensifying smog." With fewer vehicles on the road, accidents would decrease substantially, and busy city highways, like those in Chicago, Illinois, would become far more manageable. Moreover, reduced traffic would free up resources for road maintenance, ensuring roads remain in better condition for longer. Initiatives such as Car-Free Day have already demonstrated positive impacts in various parts of the world, and the effects could be even more profound in larger countries like the United States, creating "a good opportunity to take away stress and lower air pollution." Consequently, reducing the number of cars on our roads would benefit society in numerous ways.

Not long ago, the idea of public transportation becoming mainstream seemed remote. Today, however, it represents a viable solution for businesses and entire towns to reduce reliance on cars. As car usage declines, areas with increased foot traffic will receive greater attention and care. New sidewalks, restaurants, and shopping areas will emerge, creating vibrant urban spaces. Shifting investment away from extensive road maintenance towards public areas will boost urban development, as "parks and sports centers also have bloomed throughout the city; uneven, pitted sidewalks have been replaced by broad, smooth sidewalks. New restaurants and upscale shopping districts have cropped up." Embracing public transportation over private vehicles has the potential to significantly improve our cities.

Cars have served us well for many years. However, reducing our reliance on them, or even eliminating them altogether, is now a feasible option given the potential for reduced emissions, fewer cars on the road, and improved urban environments. While the task of reducing car dependence may seem challenging, the infrastructure is already in place. Public transportation systems exist in major cities such as New York and Chicago and can be readily implemented in smaller towns and communities. The considerable benefits, such as cleaner air and enhanced urban spaces, far outweigh any minor inconveniences. Adopting a strategy of "smart planning," which "separates suburban life from auto use, as a component of a movement called smart planning" represents the most effective path forward to capitalize on the advantages of reduced car usage, both for current and future generations.

While opinions on driving vary, an increasing number of countries are implementing restrictions on car usage, or outright bans. This shift presents more benefits than drawbacks.

Cars contribute significantly to air pollution, which in turn damages the atmosphere. Reducing car dependency can halt or lessen this pollution. While air pollution may persist, its intensity could be substantially diminished. Furthermore, individuals would be freed from the financial burdens associated with car ownership, such as purchasing vehicles and fuel. Alternatives exist that can effectively minimize our reliance on cars.

Sociologists suggest that reduced car travel could have a notably positive impact on carbon emissions and the environment. As one of the largest contributors to emissions, second only to power plants, transportation's footprint could be significantly lessened. Therefore, by consciously choosing to limit our car usage, we are essentially acting in our own best interests by contributing to a healthier planet.

Another potential advantage of this approach is reduced financial strain on communities. People often allocate considerable sums to gasoline, new vehicles, and associated costs, funds that could be better utilized elsewhere.

Many car trips are unnecessary, undertaken simply out of habit. Teenagers, for instance, frequently use cars for socializing or commuting to work, when other transportation options might suffice. This isn't a call to eliminate car usage entirely, but rather an encouragement to reconsider unnecessary trips. For short distances, walking offers a healthier and more sustainable alternative.

Elisabeth Rosenthal, author of "The End of Car Culture," notes that her own adult children, ages 19 and 21, "have not even bothered to get a driver's license...." They have adapted to navigate the world using alternative methods, organizing "their summer jobs and social life around where they can walk or take public transportation or carpool with friends." This highlights the viability of alternative transportation models. Carpooling, for instance, can reduce both traffic congestion and individual expenses.

In conclusion, there are substantial advantages to limiting car use, with the potential for even more benefits as we adapt our lifestyles.

Rewritten Text:
It's election day – the Tuesday following the first Monday of November, a day when, every four years, the vast majority of Americans head to the polls to select a new President. But does our vote truly decide the outcome? While we live in a democracy that ostensibly allows us to choose our leaders, the Electoral College throws a wrench into the works. This system involves electors, chosen to represent a presidential candidate, who ultimately convene to determine the President and Vice President, with Congress tallying their votes. Each presidential candidate has a designated group of electors, typically selected by their political party. But is this who we're really voting for, and is this system equitable? The Electoral College is an outdated system and should be eliminated.

Under the current system, we don't directly elect our President. Instead, we cast our ballots for a candidate's electors. These electors are the ones who truly hold the power to decide who leads the nation, not the people. As "What Is the Electoral College?" points out, "...when you vote for your candidate you are actually voting for your candidate's electors," effectively making our votes indirect. These electors can be anyone, even individuals not holding public office. This isn't the direct democracy many voters want. In fact, according to "Electoral College: Why even the bestlaid defenses of the system are wrong," "...over 60 percent of voters would prefer a direct election to the kind we have now," a significant majority expressing dissatisfaction with the status quo.

Furthermore, the distribution of electoral votes is fundamentally unequal. Larger states naturally possess more electoral votes than smaller states, such as Maryland. California, due to its larger population, wields considerably more influence. While the House of Representatives manages representation proportionally, the Electoral College creates unnecessary disparity, potentially skewing election outcomes. A nation founded on the principles of equality and liberty should not tolerate such imbalances.

The Electoral College also creates unnecessary confusion. Voters may not fully understand who they are actually voting for, and they certainly lack control over how the electors ultimately cast their ballots. This can lead to frustration and disillusionment when the electors override the popular vote. The article "Electoral College: Why even the bestlaid defenses of the system are wrong," reiterates this point, stating that "Under the electoral college system, voters vote not for the president, but for a slate of electors, who in turn elect the president.", highlighting the disconnect between the popular vote and the final outcome.

Some argue that doing away with the Electoral College could lead to chaos or instability. "In defense of the Electoral College...," claims that "But each party selects a slate of electors trusted to vote for the party's nominee...however it is entirely possible that the winner of the electoral vote will not win the national popular vote." It also helps to eliminate the stress or worrying about a runoff election if nobody has a majority in votes.

In conclusion, the Electoral College is an antiquated and flawed system ill-suited for our nation. It breeds confusion, undermines the principle of one person, one vote, and risks electing a president who lacks the support of the majority. While it might offer a few minor advantages, the potential for injustice and inequity far outweighs any perceived benefits. The time has come to abolish the Electoral College and create a more fair and democratic system of electing our leaders, making America truly equal.

Rewritten Text:
The idea of limiting car usage, while seemingly restrictive, can actually lead to a less stressful lifestyle. As Heidrun Walter, a mother of two, puts it, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." The burden of car ownership extends beyond the vehicle itself, encompassing the need for parking, which can be costly, especially in developments where parking spaces can command prices of $40,000 in addition to the cost of a home. Moreover, passenger cars are significant contributors to greenhouse gas emissions, accounting for 12% in Europe and as much as 50% in the United States. Reducing car dependence offers numerous advantages, such as the convenience of walking to local shops instead of relying on highways. Decreased emissions from fewer cars would also benefit the ozone layer, while hybrid vehicles are increasingly popular as a better alternative to diesel cars. The impact of car emissions on the greenhouse effect is further influenced by weather conditions, with cold nights and warm days potentially trapping emissions and exacerbating ozone depletion.

Paris, once plagued by severe smog, implemented car restrictions, resulting in fines of €31 for violators and potential towing for persistent offenders. This measure led to a remarkable 60% decrease in traffic jams. Following the improvement in air quality, the city eventually allowed vehicles with odd-numbered plates to circulate again on Tuesdays. As Carlos Arturo remarked while riding a bicycle with his wife, "It's a good opportunity to take away stress and lower air pollution." Bogota, Colombia, has embraced car-free initiatives, creating over 118 miles of bicycle paths.

The success of Bogota's approach has inspired other cities, with Enrique Rivera, the mayor of Asuncion, Paraguay, stating, "Municipal authorities from other countries came to Bogota to see the event and were enthusiastic; these people are generating a revolutionary change, and this is crossing borders." In the United States, the number of miles driven per person had fallen by approximately 9 percent from its peak by April 2013, returning to levels seen in January 1995. This decline can be partly attributed to the recession, as economic hardship made car ownership unaffordable for some. While a bicycle may be cheaper than a car, it might be harder to get a job on one. Car expenses like gas and insurance add to the financial strain.

Interestingly, America's infatuation with cars appears to be waning. Concurrent with an increase in child population, the number of miles driven began a steady decline in 2005. The roads are dangerous enough as it is. Furthermore, fewer young people aged 16-39 are obtaining driver's licenses, although older individuals are retaining theirs longer. A study revealed a 23 percent decrease in driving among younger people between 2001 and 2009. Visionary thinkers like Bill Ford have proposed integrating various modes of transportation, including pedestrian, bicycle, and public transit, into connected networks to optimize time, conserve resources, minimize emissions, and enhance safety. In Germany, the no car rule has resulted in 70% of families not having cars, and 57% sold their car to move there. Embracing these changes can contribute to combating the greenhouse effect and preserving our planet.

Rewritten Text:
Cars are ubiquitous, serving as a primary mode of transportation for countless individuals globally. Their efficiency and utility in accomplishing daily tasks are undeniable, making them essential in modern life. However, this widespread reliance often overshadows the significant drawbacks associated with car usage. While offering some advantages, the negative consequences of car dependence arguably outweigh the benefits. These consequences include substantial environmental damage and the generation of often-unrecognized stress. Limiting our reliance on cars is a critical consideration that deserves serious attention.

The environment, which provides us with fundamental necessities like breathable air and sustenance, is increasingly threatened by car emissions. These emissions release carbon dioxide, a major contributor to air pollution. Robert Duff's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," illustrates this point vividly. Duff details how Paris's air quality deteriorated to the point where the government implemented a driving ban to combat the smog, primarily caused by diesel car emissions. This issue is exacerbated in France due to tax policies that favor diesel vehicles. According to Duff, "diesels makes up 67 percent of vehicles in France, compared to.3 percent average of diesle engins in the rest of Western Europe, according to Reuters." Duffer further explains that densely populated cities, such as Beijing, China, suffer the worst air pollution due to high car usage. Recognizing the detrimental impact of cars, countries worldwide are beginning to implement strategies to reduce their use. Elisabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes on Without Cars," features Vauban, Germany, an upscale community where residents have largely abandoned car ownership. In fact, 70% of Vauban families do not own a car, and 57% sold a car prior to moving to the community. Car ownership is still permitted but requires the purchase of a $40,000 parking space. Residents prioritize walking and cycling. Vauban serves as a model for mitigating greenhouse gas emissions from vehicles, reflecting a positive trend.

Beyond environmental harm, cars can contribute to stress levels, often without individuals being fully aware of the connection. Heidrun Walter, quoted in "In German Suburb, Life goes on without Car," stated, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Similarly, Carlos Arturo, a businessman, in "Carfree day is spinnig into a big hit in Bogota" noted that Bogotá's car-free day was "a good opportunity to take away stress and lower air pollution." These experiences highlight how cars can become central to people's lives, generating unnoticed stress until their use is reduced.

As President Barack Obama noted in "The End of Car Culture," a move away from car dependence could significantly assist the U.S. in curbing greenhouse gas emissions. Rewritten Text:
A world free from cars might appear utopian, yet this vision is gradually materializing through innovative urban experiments, driving restrictions in certain cities, and the observance of car-free days on a national scale. The increasing pollution generated by automobiles is undeniable. While we may often overlook the consequences, the reality is that our planet is suffering, necessitating urgent action.

Consider Vauban, Germany, a town with an unconventional rule: residents must either forgo car ownership or pay a substantial €40,000 parking fee. This policy has resulted in "70 percent of Vauban's families [not owning] cars, and 57 percent [having] sold their car to move here," according to one report. Heidrun Walter, a mother of two and a media trainer, explains, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." This approach, which separates residential life from car dependence, is termed "Smart Planning." Given that passenger cars account for 12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe, and up to 50 percent in the United States, Smart Planning offers a pathway to reduced emissions and cleaner, healthier air.

Paris, France, provides another illustration of car-related pollution problems. As reported by another source, "After days of near-record pollution, Paris enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city." This measure resulted in a 60 percent decrease in traffic congestion during the five-day period. The severe smog was largely attributed to diesel fuel, the primary fuel source for 67 percent of French drivers. Climatic conditions also played a role, as "cold nights and warm days caused the warmer layer of air to trap car emissions."

This shift isn't confined to Europe. Bogota, Colombia, recently celebrated its third annual Day Without Cars, during which private vehicles are prohibited, with only buses and taxis permitted. Carlos Arturo Plaza noted, "It's a good opportunity to take away stress and lower air pollution." This year marked the first participation of two additional Colombian cities, Cali and Valledupar, in the Day Without Cars initiative.

Even in America, attitudes toward car usage are changing. Although the number of miles driven in the US peaked in 2005, there has been a steady decline in recent years. "As of April 2013, the number of miles driven per person was nearly 9 percent below the peak and equal to where the county was in January 1995." While this decrease benefits the environment, it also presents challenges for the automotive industry. Companies like Ford and Mercedes are proactively adapting by rebranding themselves as "mobility" providers. Furthermore, New York City's successful bikesharing program, increased construction of bike lanes, and adjustments to tunnel tolls reflect this shifting landscape.

The environmental damage caused by cars demands immediate correction. With the emergence of these eco-friendly and car-reducing strategies, the prospect of a life less dominated by automobiles is drawing closer.

Rewritten Text:
Navigating the complexities of the Electoral College debate presents a significant challenge. While the system boasts a deep-rooted history, established by the nation's founders, its original justification seems increasingly outdated. The Electoral College emerged when the average American was perceived as a simple, less-educated farmer, supposedly incapable of making informed electoral decisions. Today, however, the landscape has drastically shifted. Public education is far more advanced, and a wealth of readily available media outlets offer comprehensive information on candidates and their platforms. Considering the president's primary duty is to serve and protect the American people, shouldn't those same people have the direct power to choose their leader, rather than a convoluted system of electors?

Even staunch supporters of the Electoral College concede its inherent complexity. A citizen's vote ostensibly goes to a slate of electors pledged to a specific candidate, these electors then cast the actual vote for president. As Bradford Plumer argues in "The Indefensible Electoral College," voters lack control over their electors' choices, and confusion often leads voters to miscast their ballots. Furthermore, history reveals instances of "faithless electors" defying their pledges, undermining the very principle of representation.

The Electoral College's winner-take-all approach also leads to a skewed focus on "swing states," where neither party holds a firm majority. Candidates disproportionately concentrate their resources on these pivotal states, neglecting areas where their party faces a strong opposition. A Democratic candidate might avoid campaigning in a predominantly Republican state, knowing they are unlikely to win and, consequently, gain any electoral votes. This contrasts sharply with a popular vote system, where every vote contributes to the final count, incentivizing candidates to engage with voters across the entire nation.

In contrast, electing a president by popular vote offers a more balanced and equitable approach, ensuring that even minority parties in non-swing states have a voice. Studies indicate a strong preference for direct election; a Gallup poll in 2000 revealed that over 60% of voters favored it. Even Richard A. Posner, a defender of the Electoral College, admits the potential for a tie, underscoring the system's inherent risks. Posner's claim that "no voter's vote swings a national election" rings hollow; if universally believed, it would dissuade anyone from voting. The individual voter *does* matter, as do the minority parties often marginalized by the Electoral College. A system based on popular vote is the fairest way to elect a president who truly represents and serves all Americans – a government "of the people, by the people, for the people." Let all the people vote.

The system's capacity for such catastrophic outcomes demands its abolishment.

Beyond the disaster potential, the electoral college lacks direct representation. "When you vote for your candidate you are actually voting for your candidate's electors," according to the Office of the Federal Register in "What Is the Electoral College?". This indirect process deprives voters of direct control over who governs the country. Plumer rightly asserts that "At the most basic level, the electoral college is unfair to voters" and deems it "unfair, outdated, and irrational." Moreover, the system incentivizes candidates to prioritize "swing" states, neglecting voters in areas deemed unwinnable, as Plumer highlights. This strategic focus on winning, rather than governing for all, raises serious concerns about the electoral college's impact on our government.

In conclusion, the electoral college presents a risk to democracy and fairness, and because of this, we should abolish the system.

We often use cars for errands, commutes, and shopping trips, but it's worth considering a world without them, a world that actually presents several benefits.

Furthermore, the absence of cars would necessitate more active modes of transportation like walking and cycling. Finally, there are indicators that car culture itself is waning. According to "The End of Car Culture," studies suggest Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less, and obtaining fewer licenses. This trend, coupled with factors like rising gas prices, the dangers of distracted driving, and a general decline in driving aptitude among some, points to a shift away from car dependence. This decreased prioritization of driving can only benefit the environment in the long run.

In summary, a world with fewer cars offers considerable advantages. Beyond the environmental benefits and improvements to public health, the shift away from car dependence is already underway. Why continue to pollute the air with car emissions when we can choose healthier, more sustainable alternatives like walking or cycling? Embracing a car-free lifestyle presents a multitude of positive outcomes.

Rewritten Text:
The Electoral College, a system used for electing the President, has been a source of ongoing debate and contention for many years. The central question revolves around whether to maintain the current system or to abolish it altogether. Some argue that the Electoral College is undemocratic, disadvantaging both voters and candidates. Conversely, others believe it represents the optimal method for presidential elections. While arguments exist on both sides, the crucial question remains: what action, if any, can be taken regarding this system, whether perceived as beneficial or detrimental?

Essentially, the Electoral College is a compromise, a balance between a popular vote system and a method of electors. Each candidate's political party chooses a slate of electors for each state. Therefore, when citizens cast their ballot for a specific candidate, they are, in effect, voting for the electors pledged to that candidate.

So, why is there a push to eliminate the Electoral College entirely? The answer lies in the significant issues it presents. Perhaps the most concerning aspect is the potential for a tie in the electoral vote. How could a tie occur in a presidential election? The possibility exists because electors, on occasion, might deviate from their intended purpose and vote for a different candidate. This scenario is plausible. Although voters believe they're selecting a candidate, in reality, they are choosing electors who aren't always guaranteed to vote according to their original pledge, potentially resulting in a shift in the vote count if electors don't support the party's chosen candidate. As stated in one source, "In 2000... Al Gore... won the popular vote but lost the presidency... over 60 percent of voters would prefer a direct election." This suggests a widespread discontent with the Electoral College.

Despite this sentiment, some individuals still favor retaining the Electoral College. As one source notes, "each party selects a slate of electors trusted to vote for the party's nominee, and that trust is rarely betrayed." This implies that the party-selected electors are generally reliable in supporting their party's candidate. Furthermore, regional appeal plays a role; if a presidential candidate only enjoys majority support within a specific region, they are "unlikely to be a successful president." This is because a president primarily supported by a single region may not adequately address the needs and concerns of the rest of the country, potentially hindering their chances of effective governance and re-election. Finally, when no presidential candidate achieves a clear majority, the Electoral College can provide a decisive outcome.

In conclusion, both sides of the Electoral College debate present valid and well-reasoned arguments, leaving the ultimate fate of the system uncertain. Ultimately, deciding which position you believe is correct, is up to you, because after all, this is a democracy.

Rewritten Text:
For many, the automobile is an integral part of daily life, used for everything from school drop-offs to transporting children to various activities. Yet, a growing movement advocates for reduced car dependence as a solution to pressing global issues.

The detrimental impact of car usage, particularly on the environment, is widely acknowledged. Air pollution and carbon emissions are significant concerns. Passenger cars in Europe, for instance, contribute roughly 12% of greenhouse gas emissions, a figure that can soar to 50% in car-heavy areas of the United States. Congestion exacerbates these problems. In France, smog levels spiked, largely attributed to diesel-fueled vehicles, leading to a phenomenon where warm air trapped emissions, highlighting the need to reduce toxic air circulating in our atmosphere and increase of a healthier lively one.

Beyond environmental consequences, personal well-being is also affected. Individuals worldwide have participated in car-free initiatives, often reporting positive experiences. In Vauban, Germany, for example, a resident of an upscale community, Heidrun Walkter, a mother of two, relinquished her car. She now enjoys the sounds of bicycles and children, remarking, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Limiting car use also promotes physical activity. During a car-free day in Bogota, Columbia, millions commuted by foot, bike, skates, or public transport in an effort to combat smog. The initiative not only lowered smog levels but also reduced stress. The capital city experienced traffic-free streets and fostered greater social connection. One man, cycling with his wife, noted the benefits for both the environment and their personal well-being.

In conclusion, curtailing car usage has demonstrably reduced air pollution and smog globally. It has reintroduced the sounds of children playing and bicycles whizzing by. From Germany to France, Columbia to the United States, various countries implement initiatives to mitigate the harmful effects of car dependency, yet ongoing efforts remain crucial.

Rewritten Text:
Dear State Senator,

I am writing to strongly encourage you to support the transition to a national popular vote for the presidential election. I believe this reform is crucial for three primary reasons: the potential for electors to disregard the popular vote, the alarming consequences of an Electoral College tie, and the inherent inequity of the winner-take-all system.

Firstly, the existing system allows for the possibility of electors acting against the will of the people. As Bradford Plumer points out in "The Indefensible Electoral College: Why Even the Bestlaid Defenses of the system are wrong," there have been historical instances where electors have attempted to subvert the popular vote. For example, in 1960, segregationists in Louisiana's legislature nearly succeeded in replacing Democratic electors with those opposed to President J.F.K. Moreover, electors have, on occasion, disregarded their party's candidate and cast their vote for someone else entirely.

Secondly, the prospect of a tied Electoral College vote presents a deeply concerning scenario. Should this occur, the election would be decided by the House of Representatives, with each state delegation receiving a single vote. This arrangement creates a significant imbalance, where a representative from Wyoming, representing a population of roughly 500,000, would hold the same voting power as representatives from California, who represent nearly 35 million people. The precariousness of the current system is highlighted by the fact that in 1976, a shift of just 5,559 votes in Ohio and 3,687 votes in Hawaii would have resulted in such a tie, demonstrating how close we have come to this potential crisis.

Finally, the winner-take-all approach utilized by the Electoral College fosters inequality among voters. Candidates tend to concentrate their resources and attention on swing states, neglecting states perceived as unwinnable. During the 2000 campaign, seventeen states, including Rhode Island and Southern California, were virtually ignored by the candidates. Additionally, voters in 25 of the largest media markets were not exposed to any campaign advertising. It is difficult to justify a system that places the fate of the presidency in the hands of a select group of swing state voters.

Therefore, I reiterate my strong support for changing to election by popular vote to select the president of the United States. Your vote in favor of this critical reform is essential to address the risks posed by faithless electors, the dangers of an Electoral College deadlock, and the inherent unfairness of the winner-take-all system.
